summary_text:
S:
The patient reports an episode of chest discomfort during yard work over the weekend, associated with shortness of breath and mild chest pain. He denies prior episodes of chest pain and reports no other current symptoms. He reports a previous knee surgery approximately two months ago and states he has been feeling well since then. He reports his blood pressure was elevated at the ER, around 180/95, but seemed to regulate after the visit. He has no family history of heart disease but does have a cousin with a history of heart disease. He denies chest pain with exercise since his knee surgery.

O:
Blood pressure measured 180/95 at ER presentation. Carotid bruit auscultated on the right side. Lungs are clear to auscultation bilaterally. Heart rate is 72 bpm and regular. Heart rhythm is regular. Thyroid is enlarged, but not tender. No lower extremity edema is present. EKG shows normal sinus rhythm. Echocardiogram reveals mild left ventricular dysfunction with a reduced ejection fraction.

A:
The patient's presentation suggests a possible cardiac etiology for the chest pain, potentially exacerbated by hypertension. The echocardiogram reveals mild left ventricular dysfunction, which requires further investigation.

P:
Start carvedilol 25 mg twice daily. Continue lisinopril 10 mg daily. Order a cardiac catheterization to evaluate for coronary artery disease. Schedule follow-up in one week to assess blood pressure response to the new regimen and discuss the results of the cardiac catheterization.
reference_summary:
HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Roger Nelson is a 62-year-old male who presents for emergency room follow-up for chest pain. The patient was doing yard work over the weekend when he began to feel short of breath and a full chest pain for approximately hour. He denies a history of chest pain. The patient notes that after he finished he felt fine. He has not had any other symptoms since that time. He denies a family history of heart disease.

The patient underwent right knee surgery a couple of months ago and has been feeling well since then. He has been in rehab and recovery. He denies chest pain while doing exercises in physical therapy for his knee.

His blood pressure was noted to be 180/95 mmHg when he visited the emergency room. He notes that his blood pressure has not been as high as usual.

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Neck
• General Examination: I do feel a little large thyroid that is not tender. She has a carotid bruit on the right side. 

Respiratory
• Auscultation of Lungs: Clear bilaterally. Cardiovascular
• Auscultation of Heart: Regular rate and rhythm, but I do hear 3/6 systolic ejection murmur. Gastrointestinal
• Examination of Abdomen: Soft. There is a healed scar on the right knee from prior knee surgery. Musculoskeletal
• Examination: There is no lower extremity edema.

RESULTS

Blood pressure is elevated.

EKG is normal.

Echocardiogram demonstrates decreased ejection fraction.

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

The patient is a 62-year-old male who presents for emergency room follow-up. He presented to the emergency room for chest pain and elevated blood pressure of 180/95. His EKG from the emergency room was normal, but ejection fraction on echocardiogram was abnormal. His blood pressure today was still elevated.

Chest pain and hypertension.
• Medical Reasoning: Given his recent episode of blood pressure elevation and chest pain, I suspect an element of coronary artery disease as well as pumping dysfunction.
• Medical Treatment: We will change his blood pressure regimen to carvedilol 25 mg twice a day. He should also continue lisinopril 10 mg a day and I want to see how his blood pressure does on that regimen. I also recommended cardiac catheterization on him to make sure that he does not have any blockages in his heart.

Patient Agreements: The patient understands and agrees with the recommended medical treatment plan.

section_miscategorizations.text: Blood pressure measured 180/95 at ER presentation.
section_miscategorizations.reason: In the Subjective section, this ER blood pressure is explicitly reported as something the patient states ("He reports his blood pressure was elevated at the ER, around 180/95"). In the Objective section it is restated as an observed vital without attribution, which could imply it was directly measured at this visit. Since this value is historical and patient-reported rather than a current clinician-measured vital, it more properly belongs in S; however, the misplacement is minor and not substantially misleading.
